verdict_reasoning: ATP5F1B is primarily a mitochondrial inner-membrane F1-catalytic-core protein; however, documented evidence shows translocation to the cell surface under specific non-baseline conditions: (1) tumor-like acidic and hypoxic microenvironments, (2) cancer cells (multiple cancer types), (3) endothelial cells under hemodynamic stress or hypercholesterolemia. Translocation is stress/state-induced, not baseline. The protein reaches the outer leaflet via mitochondrial-to-surface translocation in response to oncogenic transformation and metabolic stress. Cell surface ATP5B engages in multiple biological processes (viral entry, T-cell recognition, extracellular ATP generation, metastasis) and is recognized as a therapeutic target in cancer. This qualifies as cell_state_induced contextual surface accessibility.
